metadata.summary: Identify, prioritize, and validate features using 4 signal sources, Kano classification, RICE scoring, and fake-door testing.

content.title: Feature Discovery
content.summary: A methodology for identifying, prioritizing, and validating product features before committing engineering effort. The core rule: collect feature signals from 4 sources (customer research, usage analytics, competitive analysis, market trends), classify each using the Kano model, score top candidates with RICE (Reach × Impact × Confidence / Effort), and validate the top-3 with a fake-door or prototype test before building.

why.p: Feature decisions based on loudest-customer requests or competitor copying lead to bloat, unused code, and missed adoption drivers. RICE scoring makes trade-offs explicit and defensible. Kano classification prevents conflating must-have fixes with excitement features. Validating before building eliminates the most common cause of wasted engineering cycles.

list.item: Pre-roadmap planning when the feature backlog has more than ~30 candidates.
After a noticeable churn or activation drop, to decide between fixing flows and adding capability.
When stakeholders push competing feature requests and you need a defensible ranking.
When validating a new feature idea before committing engineering.
Pre-PMF: run problem-validation first; there is no stable user base to score Reach against.
Single-feature decisions where the cost of running RICE exceeds the cost of just building.
Fewer than 5 features in scope — prioritization overhead exceeds value.
Telemetry is missing — RICE on guessed Reach is theatrical.

section.name: feature-signal-sources-and-categorization

list.item: Customer research: interviews, support tickets, feature requests, churn interviews, NPS detractor follow-ups.
Usage analytics: feature adoption rates, drop-off points, power user behavior, time-in-feature, error rates.
Competitive analysis: competitor features, competitor gaps, competitor review sentiment, new entrant patterns.
Market trends: industry reports, technology changes, regulatory changes, adjacent market cross-pollination.

rule.statement: Weight support tickets and feature requests by customer ARR or segment, not by volume; vocal users are not necessarily your most valuable users.
rule.rationale: A free-tier user who requests a feature 10 times has less signal value than a high-ARR customer who mentions it once. Volume-based weighting produces a roadmap optimized for your noisiest, least valuable segment.

rule.statement: Do not add competitor features without understanding the job that feature serves; feature parity copying leads to bloat without retention benefit.
rule.rationale: Competitors build features for their customers, not yours. Copying without understanding the job produces features that fit neither audience.

section.p: Kano Model: Classify every candidate feature using Kano before scoring; Must-have (threshold), Performance (more is better), Excitement (delight), or Indifferent.

list.item: Must-have (Threshold): expected baseline; absence causes dissatisfaction; presence does not impress. Example: login and security.
Performance (More is better): satisfaction scales linearly with quality. Example: speed, reliability.
Excitement (Delighter): unexpected feature that generates strong positive response. Example: AI-powered suggestions.
Indifferent: neither presence nor absence affects satisfaction.

rule.statement: Classify every candidate feature using Kano before scoring; Must-have (threshold), Performance (more is better), Excitement (delight), or Indifferent.
rule.rationale: A Must-have feature that is missing causes dissatisfaction regardless of how many Excitement features are present. Kano prevents the mistake of adding delighters while leaving a basic expectation unmet.

rule.statement: Run Kano classification per customer segment, not on the whole user base; the same feature is a Must-have for one segment and Indifferent for another.
rule.rationale: Aggregate Kano surveys hide segment-specific needs. A feature that is Indifferent on average may be critical for your highest-value segment.

section.p: Opportunity Scoring (ODI): Calculate Opportunity = Importance + (Importance - Satisfaction) for each outcome; target features where Importance is high and Satisfaction is low.

rule.statement: Calculate Opportunity = Importance + (Importance - Satisfaction) for each outcome; target features where Importance is high and Satisfaction is low.
rule.rationale: The ODI formula surfaces underserved outcomes. High importance with high satisfaction means customers are already served. High importance with low satisfaction is the opportunity.

example.title: Opportunity Scoring example
example.code:
Feature outcome: "View dashboard with just 3 key metrics" Importance (N=15 interviews): 9/10 Satisfaction with current solution: 4/10 Opportunity = 9 + (9 - 4) = 14 (high priority)
Feature outcome: "Export to CSV" Importance: 6/10 Satisfaction: 7/10 Opportunity = 6 + (6 - 7) = 5 (low priority)

rule.statement: Score every candidate feature with RICE = (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort; require a telemetry source or interview citation for every input value.
rule.rationale: RICE without evidence is guesswork with false precision. Confidence% without a cited source should be forced to null; Reach without telemetry should be dropped back to discovery.

list.item: Reach: users affected per quarter (must come from telemetry, not interviews).
Impact: magnitude — 0.25 (minimal), 0.5 (low), 1 (medium), 2 (high), 3 (massive).
Confidence: evidence level — 50% (weak), 80% (moderate), 100% (strong; requires specific metric or interview ID).
Effort: person-weeks to build and ship.

rule.statement: Lock Effort estimates in a separate engineering review before running RICE; engineers inflate effort to kill features they dislike if effort is open during scoring.
rule.rationale: RICE is a prioritization tool, not a commitment. Keeping effort estimation separate from the scoring session removes political pressure from the Effort input.

list.item: XS: trivial, less than 1 day.
S: small, 1-3 days.
M: medium, 1-2 weeks.
L: large, 2-4 weeks.
XL: very large, 1-3 months.

rule.statement: Validate the top-3 RICE features with a fake-door test or prototype before committing engineering; empirical CTR beats Confidence% from scoring.
rule.rationale: Fake-door tests directly measure user intent. A 10% click rate on a "coming soon" button is stronger evidence than an 80% Confidence estimate in a RICE spreadsheet.

list.item: Fake door: low effort, medium confidence — surface a button/link for the feature and measure clicks.
Prototype: medium effort, high confidence — clickable mockup, measure task completion.
Wizard of Oz: medium effort, high confidence — simulate the feature manually; user doesn't know it's not built.
Limited beta: high effort, very high confidence — ship to a small segment and measure retention delta.
Pre-sales: medium effort, very high confidence — charge for the feature before it's built.

antipattern.description: Agent fabricates Reach values when telemetry is missing.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated Reach inflates RICE scores for unverified features. Require a data source link for every Reach value; use null when no source exists.

antipattern.description: Agent collapses distinct jobs ("export PDF" and "share PDF") into one feature cluster.
antipattern.reason: Collapsing distinct jobs loses the granularity needed to assign effort correctly and build the right version. Require sample ticket IDs per cluster so humans can audit the grouping.

antipattern.description: Using Kano classification from LLM-only input as the final call.
antipattern.reason: LLM Kano classification is unreliable without survey data. Use it for shortlisting only; require user survey data before finalizing the classification.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive feature discovery as a four-stage pipeline: collect (analytics/tickets/interviews), categorize (Kano), score (RICE+ODI), validate (fake-door/prototype). Each stage is a separate subagent run with structured-output JSON so the next stage can consume it. Keep the human in the loop at two checkpoints: confidence values before RICE, and the validation method choice. Persist the feature-request log as a single markdown table the agent can append to over weeks.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drive the discovery board template through SDD lifecycle and emit structured outputs per step.
nero-sdd-executor-agent — same workflow inside NERO context for internal tooling features.
A custom feature-discovery-analyst subagent (sonnet) for ticket clustering and pattern extraction; haiku for filling RICE rows once Reach/Effort are pinned by humans.

section.p: Prompt pattern: Role: feature-discovery-analyst. Input: support_tickets.jsonl (1k rows), analytics_summary.md, interviews.md. Task: cluster tickets by underlying job; output JSON {clusters:[{job, count, severity, sample_ticket_ids}]}. Constraint: do not propose solutions, only surface jobs. Min cluster size: 5 tickets.

Role: rice-scorer. Input: candidate_features.json (with confirmed Reach + Effort), product context. Task: assign Impact (0.25/0.5/1/2/3) and Confidence (50/80/100); output JSON with rationale per row. Constraint: cite a metric or interview quote per row; refuse to score without evidence.

list.item: Lock the Effort estimate via a separate eng-only review BEFORE the agent runs RICE; otherwise eng inflates effort to kill features they dislike.
Reach must come from telemetry, not interviews. If you cannot measure it, drop the feature back to discovery.
Validate top-3 RICE features with fake-door before committing — empirical CTR beats Confidence%.
Run Kano per segment, not on the whole base — the same feature is a Must-have for one segment and Indifferent for another.
Keep the discovery board immutable per quarter; new ideas wait for next cycle. Prevents agent thrash on re-scoring.
Track "Rejected" with reason — saves re-litigating the same idea every quarter.

template.purpose: Quarter-level discovery board: active collection sources, feature ideas table, Kano classification, RICE scores, validation plan.
Per-feature log: metadata, problem statement, impact assessment, validation checkboxes, decision + reasoning.
